proven.claim: PROVEN: early return false is sound — fewer than 2 asserts cannot form a trivial identity
proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5
; LINE: 94
; PROVEN: early return false is sound — fewer than 2 asserts cannot form a trivial identity
; A trivial identity requires (assert P) + (assert (not P)) → at least 2 asserts

(declare-const assert_count Int)
(declare-const result Int)             ; 1 = true, 0 = false
(declare-const is_genuine_trivial Int) ; 1 = formula truly has P and ¬P

; Code transition: early return branch
(assert (< assert_count 2))
(assert (= result 0))

; Domain invariant: a genuine P ∧ ¬P pattern needs at least one equality assert
; and one negation assert — that is, at least 2 assert statements
(assert (=> (= is_genuine_trivial 1) (>= assert_count 2)))

; Negate the property: can the formula be a genuine trivial identity
; when assert_count < 2 and result = 0?
(assert (= is_genuine_trivial 1))
(check-sat)

proven.claim: PROVEN: when atomic equality and matching negation are both regex-captured,
proven.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5
; LINE: 94
; PROVEN: when atomic equality and matching negation are both regex-captured,
; the function correctly returns true (the commuted-pair check is exhaustive
; for the two-token case)

(declare-const eq_pair_stored Int)   ; 1 = "A B" entered equalities list
(declare-const neg_pair_found Int)   ; 1 = "A B" or "B A" in negations list
(declare-const result Int)           ; 1 = true, 0 = false

; Code transition: equality regex matched, pair pushed to equalities
(assert (= eq_pair_stored 1))

; Code transition: loop checks negations.includes(eq) OR negations.includes(b+" "+a)
; if either holds, returns true immediately
(assert (= neg_pair_found 1))

; Code transition: match found in loop body → result = true
(assert (=> (and (= eq_pair_stored 1) (= neg_pair_found 1)) (= result 1)))

; Negate: can result = false despite both sides matching?
(assert (= result 0))
(check-sat)

violations.claim: REACHABLE: isTrivialIdentity returns false for a genuine trivial identity
violations.smt2:
; PRINCIPLE: P5
; LINE: 94
; REACHABLE: isTrivialIdentity returns false for a genuine trivial identity
; when assertions use compound S-expression arguments.
;
; Example formula:
;   (assert (= (+ x 1) y))
;   (assert (not (= (+ x 1) y)))
;
; This is genuinely P ∧ ¬P and is trivially unsat, but the equality regex
;   ^\(assert\s+\(=\s+(\S+)\s+(\S+)\)\)$
; requires exactly two atomic tokens. The argument "(+ x 1)" contains internal
; whitespace, so (\S+) captures only "(+" and the line fails the full-line anchor.
; Both lists remain empty → no overlap → function returns false.

(declare-const assert_count Int)
(declare-const has_compound_args Int)  ; 1 = equality args are compound S-exprs e.g. (+ x 1)
(declare-const eq_list_size Int)       ; entries added to equalities list
(declare-const neg_list_size Int)      ; entries added to negations list
(declare-const result Int)             ; 1 = true, 0 = false

; Code transition: at least 2 asserts — early-return branch NOT taken
(assert (>= assert_count 2))

; Code transition: compound arguments bypass the equality regex
; (\S+) captures only up to first whitespace → wrong token boundary
; → full-line anchor \)\)$ fails → no match → nothing pushed to equalities
(assert (= has_compound_args 1))
(assert (=> (= has_compound_args 1) (= eq_list_size 0)))

; Code transition: same regex failure for negation pattern
(assert (=> (= has_compound_args 1) (= neg_list_size 0)))

; Code transition: the overlap loop over equalities is vacuous when eq_list_size = 0
; → function falls through to return false
(assert (=> (= eq_list_size 0) (= result 0)))

; Violation condition: genuine P ∧ ¬P exists but result = false
(assert (= result 0))
(check-sat)
